unitTitle: GCSE Impacts of technology (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: How does technology impact us? — the five impact areas and data protection
lessons.objectives:
I can name the five areas of impact (legal, ethical, cultural, environmental, privacy)
I can match a real example to the right area of impact
I can explain what a data subject, data controller and the ICO are
I can explain what the Data Protection Act asks organisations to do
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: legal · ethical · cultural · environmental · privacy · data subject · data controller · ICO. TA: everyone has the starter and activity worksheets open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–7) Starter — the shock headline (10 million students' data leaked). Pupils TICK the consequences on the starter worksheet (no writing). • Support: choose the missing impact area from a word-choice. • Core: name two areas from memory. • Challenge: say which area the leak is, and why.
2. (7–22) I-do → we-do — the five areas of impact + their meanings. Then the card-sort: drag five real examples into their areas (activity worksheet). • Support: read each example aloud first. • Core: sort unaided. • Challenge: name a second area each example could fit. Likely error: solar farm sorted as “legal” → fix-words “ask what it affects MOST — here it is the planet.”
3. (22–38) Data Protection Act — data subject / data controller / ICO (matching widget), then the six principles. • Support: matching term↔meaning. • Core: explain “adequate, relevant, not excessive” in plain words. • Challenge: why Sophia's farm must not keep data too long.
4. (38–50) You-do — apply a principle to Sophia's farm; capture the best answer + a photo on the show-your-work area. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — 3-2-1 check; self-tick the 4 “I can…”. TA notes who met all four.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody's screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: The law, data protection and copyright — stakeholders, the right to be forgotten and software licensing
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what a stakeholder is
I can explain the right to be forgotten
I can tell legal use of work from copyright infringement
I can compare open source and proprietary software
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: stakeholder · right to be forgotten · copyright · open source · proprietary · infringement. TA: starter and activity worksheets open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — data protection cloze (fill-in-the-blank: data · subject · controller · ICO). Mark together. • Support: word-choice for “authority”. • Core: one reason data needs protecting. • Challenge: why data laws need updating.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — what a stakeholder is (anyone affected). Multi-select: who is a stakeholder when a hospital is hacked. • Support: the multi-select with traps removed by discussion. • Core/Challenge as below. Likely error: “only the owner is a stakeholder” → fix-words “anyone AFFECTED is a stakeholder.”
3. (20–32) Right to be forgotten + copyright + Creative Commons. • Support: word-choice (erase / permission). • Core: explain the right to be forgotten; one CC benefit. • Challenge: why “open source = free” is a myth; can an algorithm be copyrighted.
4. (32–48) You-do — the software card-sort (open source vs proprietary) + capture the best answer and a photo on the show-your-work area. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (48–55) Plenary — exit-ticket mini-quiz (kept for next lesson); self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; low-arousal slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: The Freedom of Information Act and the Computer Misuse Act
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what the Freedom of Information Act does
I can say when a public body can refuse an FOI request
I can name the three levels of the Computer Misuse Act
I can decide which level a case study breaks
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: Freedom of Information Act · public organisation · vexatious · computer misuse · offence. TA: worksheets open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — the duck-house FOI story; choose who FOI requests go to (public bodies). Review last lesson's exit ticket. • Support: word-choice (government). • Core: why public spending should be open. • Challenge: why your own medical records are a Data Protection matter, not FOI.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — the FOI Act: who can ask, when a body can refuse. Multi-select: reasons to refuse a request. Likely error: “embarrassing” ticked as a valid reason → fix-words “embarrassing is NOT a legal reason to refuse.”
3. (25–42) The Computer Misuse Act — three levels. Card-sort: drag six real cases under Level 1 / 2 / 3 / not a breach. • Support: word-choice (“hacking”). • Core: sort unaided. • Challenge: why the nurse case is Level 3, not Level 1. Likely error: “all hacking is Level 3” → fix-words “only DAMAGE or CHANGE is Level 3.”
4. (42–50) You-do — capture the best CMA explanation + a photo on the show-your-work area. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — quick quiz (FIA/CMA years, who can ask, is looking-only a crime); self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; low-arousal slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Cultural impacts — downtime, the digital divide and mobile technology
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what cultural impact means
I can define downtime and its effect on an organisation
I can explain the digital divide and how to reduce it
I can give positive and negative effects of mobile technology
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: cultural · downtime · digital divide · globalisation · mobile technology. Be sensitive — never compare pupils' own access or backgrounds. TA: worksheets open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–7) Starter — a paperless school; tick the things to think about. • Support: word-choice (environment). • Core: one good thing AND one problem. • Challenge: why it could be unfair, and how to fix that.
2. (7–18) I-do → we-do — what cultural impact and globalisation mean (globe image). Concrete examples (internet changed research/shopping).
3. (18–30) Downtime + the digital divide. Multi-select: who is most affected by the digital divide. Likely error: “people with the newest phones” ticked → fix-words “the divide is about who is left OUT.” • Support: word-choice (“lost” IT systems). • Core/Challenge as below.
4. (30–48) You-do — the mobile-technology card-sort (good for us / bad for us) + one way to reduce the divide; capture the best answer and a photo on the show-your-work area. • Challenge: a concrete action for older residents. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (48–55) Plenary — sticky-note “which technology do you benefit from most?” (kept for next lesson); self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; low-arousal slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Privacy and surveillance
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what can happen when personal data is online
I can match a law to what it covers
I can explain the tension between privacy and security
I can explain the social and environmental impact of social media
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: privacy · surveillance · personal data · Telecommunications Act 1984 · Investigatory Powers Act 2016. TA: worksheets open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–7) Starter — privacy statements on a 1–5 slider (opinion, uncredited). No right answer — be ready to explain. • Support: word-choice (privacy). • Core: a reason to want messages private. • Challenge: explain both sides of the privacy/security tension.
2. (7–20) I-do → we-do — privacy vs security; the four laws and what each covers. Matching widget (law↔meaning). Likely error: mixing DPA and FOIA → fix-words “DPA protects YOUR data; FOIA lets you see what PUBLIC bodies hold.”
3. (20–35) Your data on social media + social media and the environment. Multi-select: which things record data about you (trap: a paper diary). • Core: name one piece of data an app collects + one environmental harm. • Challenge: how social media can also HELP the environment.
4. (35–50) You-do — capture the best answer + a photo on the show-your-work area. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — make a privacy-settings commitment; self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; low-arousal slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Environmental impact — e-waste, energy and technology as preserver
lessons.objectives:
I can explain how technology harms the environment
I can explain how technology can protect the environment
I can explain why e-waste is harmful
I can explain why data centres use a lot of energy
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: environmental · disposal · pollution · e-waste · upgrade culture · data centre · preserver · destroyer. TA: worksheets open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — globalisation, both sides; choose a downside of worldwide trade. • Support: word-choice (world). • Core: one example of technology helping. • Challenge: why the same technology can be both good and bad.
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — technology as preserver (renewables, smart homes, sensors) then as destroyer (e-waste image; the recycling video as a teacher-played hook).
3. (20–38) E-waste + data centres + upgrade culture. Card-sort: harms vs protects. Order task: the journey of an old phone. Multi-select: why e-waste is harmful (trap: “makes phones cheaper”). Likely error → fix-words “cheaper is not a HARM.” • Core: why data centres use energy. • Challenge: how upgrade culture adds to the problem.
4. (38–50) You-do — capture the best answer + a photo on the show-your-work area. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — share a “preserver” idea; self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Homework: the environment cloze.
Differentiation on the SAME task; low-arousal slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Ethical impact — AI, algorithms and bias
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what "ethical" means
I can explain the ethical issues around AI
I can explain how algorithms can be biased
I can explain why the Facebook 2012 study worried people
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: ethics · artificial intelligence · algorithm · bias. Keep dilemmas calm and respectful — there is no single right answer. TA: worksheets open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–7) Starter — ethical questions use the word “should”; choose the keyword. • Support: word-choice (wrong). • Core: write a “should” question with no easy answer. • Challenge: why dilemmas have more than one fair answer.
2. (7–18) I-do → we-do — what “ethical” means and how it overlaps the other impacts. Matching: ethics / algorithm / bias to their meanings.
3. (18–38) AI and driverless cars (Moral Machine image; the “What is AI?” video as a teacher-played hook — AI learns by symbolic reasoning and machine learning). Multi-select: where an algorithm's bias comes from (trap: “the screen colour”). Likely error → fix-words “bias comes from the RULES and the DATA, not the hardware.” • Support: word-choice (machine). • Challenge: why the Facebook 2012 mood study was unethical.
4. (38–50) You-do — capture the best answer + a photo on the show-your-work area. Some pupils may opt out of harsher Moral Machine scenarios — that is fine. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — quick quiz (what is ethics, two ways AI learns, what beginning with B); self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Homework: revise for the test.
Differentiation on the SAME task; low-arousal slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Putting it all together — end-of-unit test
lessons.objectives:
I can name the five areas of impact
I can answer questions about the laws (legal impact)
I can answer questions about cultural, privacy and environmental impact
I can answer questions about ethical impact and give my own opinion
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: legal · cultural · privacy · environmental · ethical. Calm, exam routine. Non-specialist support is fine. TA: worksheets open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–7) Starter — match each description to its impact area (card-sort). • Support: word-choice (ethical). • Core: which area is easiest to remember and why. • Challenge: one example that fits TWO areas.
2. (7–47) End-of-unit quiz — everyone answers the SAME 31 multiple-choice questions (the folded summative); pick the one best answer each time. Shared assessment (no Support/Core/Challenge — everyone sits the same test). Calm room; movement break allowed; reassure there is no rush.
3. (47–55) Explorer task — your opinion: which technology has had the most positive or negative impact, and why (technology, stakeholders, legal points, conclusion). Capture it + a photo on the show-your-work area. Pupils who finish the test early start this.
4. Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; the teacher marks the quiz and notes gaps to revisit.
The quiz carries no level differentiation (one shared test); the L8 STARTER carries the Support/Core/Challenge for the lesson. Low-arousal slides; movement breaks are routine.
